text: One thing you can always count on with
Disney, if there's an IP out there with
even a hint of gas left in the tank,
they will ride that thing until the
wheels fall off and probably keep going
for several more miles, leaving a trail
of sparks and flaming wreckage in their
wake. And two of the biggest IPs to fall
under their dark influence during the
Fox merger were Aliens and Predator.
Both icons of pop culture that boast at
least uh two good movies each. And well,
we've already seen how that turned out
for aliens. Get away from her.
You [ __ ]
Predator, though, is a bit more
complicated. The surprise success of
Prey on Disney Plus, and damn, man, they
must have regretted not giving that one
a theatrical release seems to have
convinced them that there's plenty more
mileage to be gotten out of the Predator
franchise. And so the floodgates have
opened and the sludge is in full flow.
Case in point, Predator Badlands, an
upcoming standalone movie from Dan
Tractenberg, the same guy who directed
Prey. The first teaser dropped a few
days ago and everyone was a buzz about
one thing.
Okay, look. I know that appearances can
be deceptive, and sometimes the VFX are
a bit unfinished in shots like this and
blah blah blah, but holy [ __ ] was this
a mistake? How is it that a high-profile
movie made in 2025 somehow looks less
convincing than a mid-budget sci-fi
action flick from the 1980s? I mean,
just take a moment to look at the
original Predator from 1987. Look how
much detail there is here. Look at the
droplets of water on its skin. The
complex markings and patterns across its
face. The deep set eyes that are barely
visible. The jaw mandibles moving and
twitching. The long tentacles hanging
down around its heads. It's an
incredible piece of creature design and
complex animatronics that's just as
iconic and alien and terrifying as the
xenomorphs were. Then look at old Lumpy
here from Badlands. It looks like
something that just wandered in from the
set of Star Trek Deep Space 9. The
skin's all rubbery and out of focus.
There's no surface detail or droplets of
water or smears of dirt or anything at
all to indicate that this thing is
actually physically present. Also, why
in the name of holy [ __ ] does it have a
side shave? I mean, you understand that
those things growing out of the side of
the predator's head aren't actually hair
that can be cut and styled like human
hair, right?
Showing it in broad daylight was also
the worst possible decision because
every visual flaw and shortcoming is
exposed for the whole world to see.
Look, I'm under no illusions about the
original prosthetic either, right?
There's a very obvious reason why movie
monsters like this and the xenomorphs
are usually shown in darkness. All those
shadows and pale moonlight help to hide
the moving parts, disguise the fact that
it's basically just a stunt man in a
rubber suit, and make the whole thing
look a whole lot more intimidating than
it probably was. That's fine. It's part
of the magic of film making, and it
worked. Filmmakers back then understood
the visual limitations of the tools they
were working with and found ways to
enhance them instead of undermining
them. Anyway, I digress. Let's talk
about the trailer itself. The plot, as
far as I understand it, is that a young
predator has been banished from his home
world for being [ __ ] or something
and ends up on some remote planet where
he teams up with a whale and Utani
android to take down the biggest,
baddest monster in the area to prove
himself worthy of a place amongst his
own people again. In a nutshell, the
Predator is now the protagonist of the
movie this time around. It's a bold
strategy, Cotton. Let's see if it pays
off for him. I mean, I'll at least give
them credit for doing something a bit
different with this one. There's only so
many times you can pit a group of guys
with guns against a lone predator that
kills them off one by one while they
slowly come to grips with the enemy that
they're facing. And conceptually, it's a
neat idea to mix things up a little and
flip the dynamic here. I'm just not
entirely convinced that an 8-ft tall
monster whose entire vocabulary consists
of and [ __ ]
happens is the kind of protagonist that
audiences are likely to get behind.
Also, and this could be just a personal
thing, but I'm not sure I want to know
more about the predators as a species. I
don't really want to know what their
society or their home world looks like
or how they interact with each other or
what they get up to in their spare time.
Much like the xenomorphs, their
fascination always lay in their
mystique. Yeah, the movies would drop
little hints about larger events in a
more complex social structure, but we
were still mostly kept in the dark about
them, and that was unquestionably a good
thing. As I've said many times before,
questions like this are usually way more
interesting and compelling than any
answers a filmmaker could give. Just ask
Ridley
Scots. The attempt to merge this movie
with the Alien franchise is another red
flag for me because every time these two
IPs have tried to crossbreed, the
results have ranged from mediocre slop
to painfully bad to downright baffling.
The connection here is Thea, a Wayland
Utani android from an unknown time
period in the Alien franchise who seems
to have ended up on the same planet as
the big
lads, right? It's too early to tell what
her function in the story is or why
she's actually here, but I can't shake
the horrifying feeling that her presence
is laying the groundwork for another
Alien versus Predator movie or, God help
us, an entire cinematic universe of this
crap.
Oh man. Oh god. Oh man. The one point of
light at the end of the tunnel here is
Dan Tractenberg, who's pulling writing
and directing duties this time around
after his work on Prey. That movie might
not have been perfect, but it was a
decent stab at doing something slightly
different with the Predator franchise.
And although I think people cut it way
more slack than it probably deserved, it
was still miles better than the loose
collection of lazy callbacks and cheap
nostalgia bait that we got in Alien
Romulus. The reason I bring all this up
is that Prey also suffered from a pretty
underwhelming trailer that people were
quick to dismiss as a typical Disney
girl boss sludgefest, only for the movie
itself to turn out way better than
expected. So, I don't want to write Bad
Lands off completely here. It could
still turn out to be a decent addition
to the franchise, but man, I think it's
about time we accepted that we're never
going to recapture the magic of the 1987
original, and anything we get now is
just a pale imitation of stuff that's
already been done far better. But trust
me, as long as there's anything remotely
left to be milked out of this franchise,
Disney will find a way to make it
happen. Oh yeah, and just a heads up
